title: Financial And Advisory Support
text:
FINANCIAL AND
ADVISORY SUPPORT FOR
CLIMATE GOVERNANCE
IN TÜRKIYE

21
FINANCIAL AND ADVISORY SUPPORT FOR CLIMATE GOVERNANCE IN TÜRKIYE
“
Türkiye has made notable progress in
advancing corporate climate governance,
with sustainability reporting increasingly
recognised as a strategic business
imperative. ”
1
2
1
CHRIS TASSIS
COUNSEL, EBRD
tassisc@ebrd.com
2
HÜSEYIN KERTIS
ASSOCIATE BANKER, EBRD
kertish@ebrd.com

2 2
LAW IN TRANSITION 2025
Türkiye faces escalating climate risks, which it is proactively trying to address through
institutional and regulatory reforms and ambitious climate policies. The European
Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) plays a dual role in supporting
Türkiye’s transition to a green economy, combining substantial green financing with
strategic advisory services. This article provides an overview of the EBRD’s financing
projects in Türkiye and highlights the Bank’s advisory and policy support, with
a particular focus on corporate climate governance. The article takes a detailed look
at a successful climate governance-related advisory engagement with a key company
in the Turkish healthcare sector and concludes with a call for broader adoption of
sustainability practices at both sector and entity level.
A SNAPSHOT OF TÜRKIYE’S STRATEGIC
APPROACH TO CLIMATE-RELATED
CONSIDERATIONS1
Türkiye is a transcontinental country
strategically positioned at the crossroads of
Europe and Asia. Its diverse topography
includes extensive mountain ranges, fertile
river valleys and a complex coastline. In light
of its unique geographical and climatic
diversity, Türkiye faces multiple climate risks,
Türkiye is committed

decade.2 Some of the most pressing risks that to achieving net-zero

emissions by

freshwater, increasing risk of desertification,
rising sea levels threatening coastal areas In response to the growing impact of climate
and heightened vulnerability to agricultural change, Türkiye has undertaken a range of
productivity losses.3 These risks underscore structural reforms and policy initiatives aimed

LAW IN TRANSITION 2025
The structure of the standards is twofold,
with TSRS S1 containing general provisions
on the disclosure of sustainability-related
financial information and TSRS S2 covering
climate-related disclosures for the four
main dimensions of climate governance:
governance, strategy, risk management,
and metrics and targets. The TSRS require
sustainability reporting for companies that
meet at least two of the following criteria over
two consecutive reporting periods, with certain The EBRD has
entities – that is to say, banks and companies
invested more than
listed on Türkiye’s stock exchange, Borsa
Istanbul – required to report regardless of €22 billion
these thresholds:
in Türkiye, with
• total assets exceeding TRY 500 million
• a nnual net sales revenue of more than €2.6 billion
TRY 1 billion
in 2024 alone
• a workforce of 250 employees or more.
The first TSRS-compliant sustainability reports
are due in 2025, covering the 2024 fiscal year.

CLIMATE GOVERNANCE IN TÜRKIYE: Rönesans exemplifies how such cooperation
WHAT COMES NEXT? can strengthen climate resilience and improve
strategic foresight through greater
Türkiye has made notable progress in advancing
transparency and accountability. In the future,
corporate climate governance, with
Türkiye’s commitment, combined with targeted
sustainability reporting increasingly recognised
support from DFIs, will be key to building
as a strategic business imperative. As companies
a more sustainable and climate-resilient
respond to growing demands for transparency,
corporate ecosystem in the country.
aligning with international reporting standards
has become essential for maintaining
competitiveness and credibility. However,
further efforts are needed to expand the
implementation of robust sustainability/climate
disclosure frameworks across the corporate
sector and ensure that climate governance
is embedded not only in compliance but also
in core strategy and risk management.
